did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically an ensemble of four Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs: DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception), to classify seven kinds of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a deep learning ensembling approach using four CNNs (DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception) on the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methods (data augmentation, weighted categorical cross-entropy), reports new results (90.02% accuracy, 90.34% precision), and compares these findings against state-of-the-art models, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Implementation of Automatic Skin Lesions Diagnosis A Deep Learning Ensembling Approach
journal: Journal of Advanced Engineering and Computation
brief_summary: This study implements a computer-aided diagnosis system for classifying seven kinds of skin lesions using a deep learning ensembling approach on the HAM10000 dataset. The authors trained and fused four Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception) without prior segmentation or denoising, utilizing data augmentation and weighted categorical cross-entropy to address class imbalance. The model was evaluated on a holdout test set of 2003 images using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC. The proposed ensemble model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 90.02%, a precision of 90.34%, and an AUC of 98.4%. These results demonstrated a 3% to 4% improvement in accuracy compared to individual networks and outperformed other state-of-the-art models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC.

all_task_types.target: Seven kinds of skin lesions (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a computer-aided diagnosis system to classify seven discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model uses an ensemble of four CNNs (DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, Xception) with a softmax layer to predict class probabilities for these seven classes. The task is explicitly described as 'classification task' and 'classify seven kinds of skin lesions'.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing more than 10,000 dermoscopic images of seven kinds of skin lesions. The ground truth for more than 50% of lesions was confirmed through histopathology, while the rest was confirmed via follow-up examination, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy.
classes_used: benign keratosis (solar lentigo / seborrheic keratosis/lichen planus-like keratosis) (BKL)

use.training: Used for training four CNNs (DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, Xception) via 5-fold cross-validation. The dataset was divided into five folds to ensure enough samples for testing in minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 5-fold cross-validation process (4 folds used for training, 1 fold held out for validation/testing in each iteration).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation on a holdout test set of 2003 images (20% of the dataset) to report accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: AUC (Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of individual CNN models (VGG16, Xception, DenseNet169, ResNet152) and ensure sufficient samples for testing in minority classes.
To assess the final performance of the ensemble model and individual networks on unseen data (20% of the dataset, 2003 images).
To address class imbalance in the training data by assigning different weights to classes during the loss calculation.
To visualize and analyze the classification performance, specifically looking at true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class.

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC (Area Under Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between classes by plotting True Positive Rate against False Positive Rate.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, F1-score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

holdout_test_set_2003_images.notes: The ensemble model achieved a 3% to 4% increase in accuracy compared to individual networks. The ensemble model outperformed other state-of-the-art models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and AUC.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of four Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (more than 1.5 million images, 1000 classes)

architecture_details.pooling: Global Average Pooling used to connect the last convolutional layer to the new Fully Connected (FC) layer
architecture_details.fc_layer: New FC layer with 512 neurons (reduced from original 4096/2048 to decrease computational cost)
architecture_details.dropout: 0.5 dropout factor added after FC layers
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Averaging the outputs of the last layer (after linear activation) of each network, then selecting the highest probability

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Reduced by factor of 0.5 after 3 epochs if accuracy did not improve
training_details.training_strategy: Transfer learning; original FC layers replaced; new layers initialized with Glorot Uniform (kernel) and zeros (bias); new layers trained for 3 epochs while other layers frozen

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Ensemble Model (Ensemble of DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from original 600x450 to 256x192 using bicubic interpolation to reduce computational cost and maintain aspect ratio
Normalization of pixel values to range between zero and one by dividing each pixel by 255
Data augmentation including horizontal flipping, vertical flipping, random rotation (+/- 10 degrees), and random horizontal/vertical shifting (shift factor 0.1)
Explicit exclusion of segmentation, denoising, hair removal (e.g., DullRazor), color space transformation, and boundary removal steps

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase dataset size and reduce overfitting.
Applied during training to increase dataset size and reduce overfitting.
Images were randomly rotated in a range between +10 and -10 degrees.
Images were randomly shifted horizontally and vertically by a shifting factor of 0.1.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning ensembling approach, which fuses DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, and Xception, achieved a top-1 accuracy of 90.02% and an AUC of 98.4% on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming individual networks and other state-of-the-art models.

Ensembling the selected CNNs increased accuracy by 3% to 4% compared to the best individual network (VGG16 at 87%), demonstrating that combining models with different sensitivities to specific classes improves overall classification performance.

The ensemble model achieved higher F1-scores than individual networks for four classes (BCC, BKL, MEL, NV) and comparable performance for others, effectively leveraging the strengths of each constituent network.

The study confirms that a simple pipeline without prior segmentation, denoising, or hair removal can achieve high-precision classification of seven skin lesion types, reducing computational complexity and preprocessing steps.

Weighted categorical cross-entropy was effective in addressing class imbalance, particularly for minority classes like dermatofibroma (DF) and vascular lesion (VASC), although these classes still exhibited lower F1-scores (71% and 86% respectively) compared to majority classes like melanocytic nevus (96%).

The proposed model has high computational costs due to the large number of parameters (109,817,668) and execution time (approx. 8 seconds to load weights), which may limit its suitability for online or resource-constrained applications despite its high accuracy.

The model demonstrated robustness across different classes, with the ensemble strategy allowing it to overcome weaknesses in individual networks, particularly for classes with limited training samples.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which introduces potential population bias as the dataset is not globally representative of all skin types and demographics.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with the 'melanocytic nevus' (NV) class containing over 6000 samples while 'dermatofibroma' (DF) and 'vascular lesion' (VASC) have fewer than 200 samples, necessitating the use of weighted categorical cross-entropy.

The model achieves high accuracy (90.02%) but has very high computational costs, with approximately 109 million parameters and an estimated 8 seconds to load weights, limiting its suitability for resource-constrained or online clinical deployment.

The paper explicitly avoids preprocessing steps such as segmentation, denoising, or hair removal (e.g., DullRazor), arguing that these can introduce noise or alter image texture, though this limits the model's ability to handle images with significant artifacts like rulers or heavy hair.

There is no external validation performed; the model was only evaluated on a holdout test set from the same dataset (HAM10000), raising concerns about generalizability to other clinical settings or datasets.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the significance of the performance improvements over state-of-the-art models.

The ground truth for the HAM10000 dataset is mixed, with only >50% confirmed by histopathology, while the rest relies on follow-up, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy, which may introduce label noise.

The ensemble approach averages the outputs of four different CNNs (DenseNet169, ResNet152, VGG16, Xception), which improves accuracy by 3-4% over individual networks but increases inference complexity and latency.
